title: Congress Moves to Block F-35 Sales as Engine Deal Faces Backlash, Erdogan Courts Venezuela and Positions for NATO Summit
subhead: A bipartisan coalition in Congress ramps up efforts to block both the $700M F-110 engine sale and any F-35 return for Turkey, citing the S-400 and Hamas ties — even as Trump signals accommodation ahead of the Ankara summit. Meanwhile, Erdogan hosts Venezuela's acting president, offers to broker Ukraine-Russia talks, and faces Kurdish demonstrations demanding Öcalan's release.

sections.heading: Congressional Coalition Takes Aim at F-35 and Engine Sales to Turkey
sections.body:
A bipartisan group in Congress is mobilizing on two fronts to block US arms sales to Turkey. Representative Dina Titus (D-NV) is leading both a Joint Resolution of Disapproval to stop the $700M+ General Electric F-110 engine sale formally notified to Congress on June 24, and a separate letter to House leadership urging them to block any Turkish return to the F-35 program. The ANCA and Hellenic American organizations are lobbying in parallel, with ANCA executive director Aram Hamparian declaring: 'Arming Erdogan doesn't defend a friend. It arms and emboldens an enemy to attack our friends.'

The legal case rests on CAATSA sanctions imposed in December 2020 on Turkey's Presidency of Defense Industries for acquiring the Russian S-400, and the FY2020 NDAA which explicitly bars F-35 transfers to Turkey so long as Ankara retains the S-400. Lawmakers note Turkey has not removed the system, taken it out of service, or addressed the underlying compliance issues. Representative Chris Pappas (D-NH) said the law requires the administration to certify Turkish compliance before any sale can proceed, while Gregory Meeks (D-NY), ranking member of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, accused the administration of bypassing Congressional review without invoking emergency authority. On the Republican side, Nicole Malliotakis (R-NY) and Mike Lawler (R-NY) joined the opposition, as did Greek-American Republicans Gus Bilirakis (R-FL), Mike Haridopolos (R-FL), and Jimmy Patronis (R-FL).

However, blocking the sale faces steep odds. A resolution of disapproval must pass both chambers and survive a likely presidential veto — requiring a two-thirds supermajority. The 15-day Congressional review window for the engine sale is narrow, and Trump has shown every intention of proceeding. Asked whether he planned to bring 'gifts' for Erdogan at the NATO summit, Trump replied affirmatively, and Vice President J.D. Vance confirmed an official review is underway examining how F-35 sales could legally proceed. Israel National News reported that Israeli authorities last week publicly identified five senior Hamas officials allegedly operating from Turkish territory, adding another layer of opposition to the F-35 push.
sections.whyItMatters: The engine sale may well proceed despite Congressional objections — but the fight itself is a preview of how F-35 reintroduction will be contested. The bipartisan breadth of opposition, spanning Armenian, Greek, and pro-Israel constituencies, means any attempt to bypass the S-400 restrictions will face sustained legislative resistance.

sections.heading: Erdogan Hosts Venezuela's Acting President in Surprise Visit
sections.body:
Venezuelan acting president Delcy Rodriguez made an unannounced visit to Istanbul on Monday, meeting with President Erdogan for one-on-one and delegation-level talks. According to Al-Monitor, the discussions focused on 'reviewing the multidimensional relations between Turkey and Venezuela' and deepening cooperation. Turkish Vice President Cevdet Yilmaz, Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan, and Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar attended, while Rodriguez brought Venezuela's foreign, tourism, and foreign trade ministers.

This is Rodriguez's first major international tour since the US capture and transfer of Venezuelan president Nicolas Maduro in January. Bilateral trade stood at $448 million in 2025, with Venezuelan petroleum and mining products flowing to Turkey and Turkish food, milling goods, and mineral fuels heading the other way. Turkey and Venezuela signed memorandums of understanding in 2024 on oil, natural gas, and mining cooperation, though it remains unclear whether those can translate into deals under Venezuela's post-Maduro transition.

Erdogan was among a small group of leaders who recognized Maduro's disputed 2024 election victory and described him as a friend after his capture, though he avoided direct criticism of the US. Rodriguez's removal from OFAC's sanctions list could make Turkish-Venezuelan engagement easier, but remaining US sanctions on Venezuela mean any energy or financial deals would still require careful compliance.
sections.whyItMatters: The visit signals Turkey's intent to preserve one of its most visible Latin American partnerships through Venezuela's political transition — and Erdogan's willingness to maintain ties with Caracas despite potential friction with Washington, just days before hosting a NATO summit where Trump will demand loyalty.

sections.heading: Turkey Offers to Host Ukraine-Russia Peace Talks as Fidan Visits Moscow
sections.body:
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan announced that Turkey is prepared to host a new round of Ukraine-Russia peace negotiations, stating: 'We are ready to bring the delegations of Russia and Ukraine together at the negotiating table in our country once again.' According to Kyiv Post, citing Anadolu Agency, Fidan said Ankara is committed to ending the conflict 'through dialogue and on the basis of international law.'

The offer comes after Fidan visited Moscow on June 17-18, meeting with Russian Foreign Minister Lavrov and President Putin, where he warned against further escalation in the Black Sea and proposed a limited ceasefire on ports and energy infrastructure. Turkey previously mediated the May-June 2025 negotiations that produced a landmark prisoner exchange of 1,000 captives from each side.

Ukraine's ambassador to Ankara welcomed the offer, and President Zelensky has outlined priorities ahead of the Ankara NATO summit: advanced air defense, energy resilience, and sustained pressure on Russia. NATO Secretary General Rutte announced that allies are expected to unveil 'tens of billions of dollars' in new defense contracts at the summit, demonstrating long-term backing for Kyiv.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is positioning itself as the indispensable broker between Kyiv and Moscow once again — a role that burnishes its diplomatic credentials before the summit and gives Erdogan leverage with both Washington and Moscow. But with Russia's hardliners pressing Putin to abandon talks and Ukraine demanding security guarantees, the mediation offer may produce more optics than outcomes.

sections.heading: Trump Presses Syria on Hezbollah, Straining Turkey's Syria Calculus
sections.body:
President Trump has publicly pushed for Syria to take on Hezbollah in Lebanon, saying at the G7: 'I suggested to Israel to let Syria take care of Hezbollah. To be honest, I think they'd do a better job.' The proposal has alarmed both Lebanon and Israel, according to the Navhind Times and Washington Post. Syrian president Ahmad al-Sharaa has pushed back, telling Al Mashhad that Trump's remarks were 'misinterpreted' and that Syria's vision is 'to stop the war and address the negative effects on Lebanon and Syria' through political and economic solutions — not military intervention.

For Turkey, the episode is directly consequential. Ankara is al-Sharaa's main state backer, and Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said in March that Turkey had interceded to defuse border tensions between Syria and Hezbollah. The Guardian reports that Turkey and Israel are each seeking to limit the other's influence in Syria, with top Israeli security officials convening a meeting on the subject this week. Erdogan said on June 10 that Israel's attacks on Syria and Lebanon had reached a point where they 'also threaten Turkey.'

Randa Slim of the Stimson Center called Trump's proposal 'at best, driven by a profound ignorance of the dynamics on the ground,' noting that Syrian forces 'include thousands of foreign jihadi fighters of uncertain loyalty and discipline' and that Syria is focused on rebuilding, not opening a new front.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's offhand push for a Syria-Hezbollah confrontation puts Turkey in a bind: it backs the new Syrian government and wants stability on its southern border, but cannot endorse a Syrian military adventure in Lebanon that could further destabilize the region. Erdogan's warning that Israel's attacks threaten Turkey reflects how directly Ankara feels the spillover.

sections.heading: Kurds Rally in Diyarbakir for Öcalan's Release as Peace Process Stalls
sections.body:
Several thousand people rallied in the Kurdish-majority city of Diyarbakir on Sunday, calling for the release of jailed PKK leader Abdullah Öcalan, according to MSN. The demonstration comes amid a prolonged stalemate in Turkey's Kurdish peace process, with no visible progress on negotiations between Ankara and the PKK since Öcalan's messages from prison earlier this year called for a political resolution.

The rally also carries regional implications. The Guardian reported this week that a longstanding US plan to deploy Kurdish fighters against Iran was shelved in part because of 'strong personal opposition from President Erdogan', who persuaded Trump to reconsider after Israeli warplanes had already begun striking Iranian border posts to open a corridor for Kurdish forces. Only 'a few hundred' fighters were available for immediate deployment, and Kurdish leaders were wary of the US after what they saw as a betrayal in Syria in January, when Washington backed a deal placing Kurdish civilian and military authorities under central Syrian government control.
sections.whyItMatters: The Diyarbakir rally is a reminder that the Kurdish question remains unresolved domestically, even as Turkey's intervention against a US-Kurdish plan against Iran shows how Erdogan's stance on Kurdish groups operates on two tracks: suppressing domestic Kurdish political demands while blocking any independent Kurdish military action that could reshape the regional balance.

sections.heading: NATO Summit Preview: Trump Demands Loyalty as Europe Prepares for Reduced US Role
sections.body:
With the Ankara NATO summit set for July 7-8, the contours of a difficult gathering are coming into focus. NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte presented Trump with charts labeled 'Trump Trillions' showing that European allies and Canada have added $1.2 trillion in defense spending since Trump first took office, along with billions in orders to US defense companies. Trump appeared uninterested, saying he simply wants loyalty: 'Give us a little nudge, give us a little kiss. We don't want much.'

Trump also expressed frustration that some NATO allies distanced themselves from the Iran war, refused or delayed access to air bases, and did not support his call to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Rutte countered that 4,000-5,000 US planes flew from European bases during the six-week conflict, but Trump dismissed the point: 'We were let down.'

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced a six-month Pentagon review of American forces in Europe, saying some countries would 'fail' the test and others would 'pass with flying colours.' Meanwhile, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz hosted the leaders of France, Italy, Poland, and the UK — the E5 — in a pre-summit coordinating meeting, underscoring European anxiety about the trajectory of US commitment.

For Erdogan, the summit is both a platform and a risk: he told AK Party members the gathering would make Ankara 'a centre of global diplomacy,' but the domestic crackdown — 178 detained, 13-day protest ban, dozens of journalists denied accreditation — is drawing international scrutiny that could overshadow the diplomatic pageantry.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's demand for loyalty rather than spending charts, combined with a formal review of US force posture in Europe, signals that the Ankara summit will be less about alliance cohesion and more about transactional recalibration — with Erdogan trying to extract maximum benefit from hosting while managing the fallout from his domestic crackdown.

bottomLine: The $700M engine sale for Turkey's KAAN fighter is inching toward approval, but the Congressional fight over F-35 access — led by Rep. Dina Titus with bipartisan backing — is a preview of the much harder battle ahead. Trump's praise for Erdogan ('everything I've ever asked him for, he's done') and his demand for NATO loyalty set the terms for the Ankara summit: transactional, not institutional. Erdogan is playing multiple boards simultaneously — courting Venezuela's post-Maduro leadership, offering Ukraine-Russia mediation, backing al-Sharaa's Syria against Trump's Hezbollah gambit, and blocking US-Kurdish plans against Iran — all while cracking down at home and advancing EEZ claims against Greece. The question for July 7-8 is whether the diplomatic choreography holds together or the contradictions show through.
sources: Reuters, Armenian Weekly, Protothema, Israel National News, Al-Monitor, Kyiv Post, Anadolu Agency, Navhind Times, Washington Post, The Guardian, MSN, RTE, Cyprus Mail, Daily Sabah
